speaker: interviewer
text: I won't. This is tape number three with Joseph Roos in Los Angeles, tape number three. OK, I think when we ended at the last tape, you were discussing the information that you were getting, and you were talking about your experience in Chicago. How long did you stay in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left Chicago in 19-- probably in the spring of 1934. And I left Chicago going for to do a story, a criminal story, a man accused of murder. It was a German aristocrat who was accused of having killed his wife. And there were two sons who lived in Los Angeles. And sent me to get to those two sons, figuring being able to talk German, so that I can loosen those two sons up, hoping to find something that would give them some sensational front page headlines. That's how I got to Los Angeles. Some time earlier the leader of the Bund in Los Angeles and the West Coast spoke to the Nazis in Chicago. And among other of things, the man said that the Jewish-- the dirty Jew lawyers are giving us trouble in Los Angeles. He went into some details, which I'll skip.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got that report from one of our undercover guys, I went to my aunt, who was a president of a synagogue in Chicago. I said, look, will you talk to your rabbi? Find out whether he knows a rabbi in Los Angeles to whom I can talk. So in due time, I got the name of the rabbi to whom I could talk. He's like, call Rabbi Edgar Magnin at Wilshire Boulevard Temple. The telephone number is such and such. So I called him and told him what I was doing. And I told him what this Nazi leader from Los Angeles was telling to the Nazis in Chicago. So I asked him, do you know anything about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yes. We have a little committee. We call it the community committee. This committee is concerned about some of the Nazi activities. He described them to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe those? Can you describe them for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me-- in a-- in-- in a second. What happened at Los Angeles was-- what happened in Los Angeles was two things. The Nazis published a nasty, mean article, just a full page type-- no, printed article about everything nasty that you or anybody can possibly think up and inserted copies of it in the As the copies came off the printing press-- you must remember at that time, the printed paper is much slower than they are doing nowadays. This is one thing that got the Jewish community excited. Here they're reading, opening the That was one thing. The other thing was they were concerned-- leaders of the Jewish community who were primarily looking after social welfare, they looked, made sure that there is a hospital where Jewish doctors can practice because it was not easy for Jewish doctors to get into a regular hospital. They looked after the cemetery. They helped the people who needed to adjust themselves, coming from Russia or anywhere else in Europe, but particularly Russia and places like that. It was called the Federation of Jewish Welfare Organizations at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these people were concerned about this. What are they going to do about it? Then here, too, in Los Angeles, they had an organization similar to the federation, which I described as a federation, in Chicago. You need to look this way. If you can look this way toward me, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so they had a little bit of a headquarter, a place where they could eat weenies-- you know, with little Germans you would say weenies, hamburger, lots of beer, and so on, and so forth, and a place where they could do a little gymnastics. So that association or that federation had a regular election of officers. The local Nazis claimed that this is an organization that has the right to be in there. That this is an organization. They faked-- they created fake organizations in order to have more delegates that could then elect their kind of people to be presidents and vice presidents in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the people belonging to that federation or association didn't like it. And they said to themselves, since Nazis don't like Jews, we should do it to the Jews. You know, your enemies, my friends, my friends, your enemies and so on. They looked for a Jewish lawyer to help them. And since they found under attorneys, a man by the name of Louis Greenbaum, or Greenbaum. They went to him with their problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this guy then went to that little Jewish group that I described. So they were concerned. They handled that case, and the Nazis were defeated in this thing. So all of this was the beginning of what later became the thing that I worked for full time. Now you wanted me to say something else. And now I've deserted you. I forgot it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted you to describe for me what were some of the things that you had been told that the Nazis had been doing. That's what I wanted you  to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the Nazis doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, here locally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, they simply fought whichever way they-- incidentally, one more thing-- I should back up a little bit. At that time, the carried classified ads listing open jobs. And the last line, not always but frequently, was 'whites and gentiles only.' So this little group was concerned with that and negotiated for quite some time with the bosses in order to get them to refuse to accept ads like that. They did all of this before I appeared on the scene. Now I came on the scene in order to do that story, getting some material out of the German-- the sons of that aristocrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next once you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once I got there, I met with one of the people who belonged to that little group. Somebody took me. This is the guy who phoned from Chicago. So we met. And this fellow was connected with the motion picture industry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And said, look, there is a cocktail party in the home of Carl Laemmle. Carl Laemmle was the founder and the president of Universal Studios. Would you like to go along this coming Sunday? I said, sure. So I met Carl Laemmle, Sr.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laemmle came from a little town in southern Germany. He was the main-- what should I say-- contributor to that little town. The main street was called Laemmle Strasse. The library was called Laemmle Library. And he had a lot of relatives by the name of Laemmle. And all of a sudden, Mr. Hitler comes to power. And Laemmle Strasse was no longer Laemmle Strasse. It was Hitler Library or Hitler Strasse. And he brought many of his immediate relatives out to Los Angeles. And they were all put on the payroll.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he enjoyed talking to me about the whole Nazi business. And the wind up was at the cocktail party, I want to come to work for me. I think the job he created, he wanted because he liked me, and he loved to go out to the beach and walk up and down at the beach and talk about his hometown and what has become of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was not a real job. I think-- I should think up new themes for new films that would be up to date at a given time. For instance, today might be the 200th anniversary of something or other. Build a whole film around it. I think it was strictly that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to work-- went back home, gave up my bachelor apartment and came out to Los Angeles. Carl Laemmle, Sr.-- Carl Laemmle, Jr. Got credit got credit as the producer for-- of which won practically every Academy Award there was. And he was very young. He was just about as old as I.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- everybody said, oh, that guy. He only knows girls. He doesn't know how to produce. And it hurt him, so we became close friends. And then there was a rumor going around at the studio that Carl Laemmle, Sr. Would sell the studio. And by then I had gotten to know a few people. Among them was a man who was then the story editor for Mary Pickford and Jesse Lasky at United Artists. And he needed an assistant. He offered me a job, so I left Universal and went to work for United Artists. And Jesse Lasky was one of the early producers who was one of the founders of Paramount. And so I went to work for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now these people that I described as a little group were after me, practically, from the beginning. We know from what we heard from our friends in Chicago what you did in Chicago. Won't you come to work for us? The leader of the group was a man by the name of Mendel B. Silberberg-- S-I-L-B-E-R-- not 'silver,' but 'Silberberg.' And he was after me, Joe, going, well, why do you want to fool around with the movies? Here's something more important to do. So I said, no. I then organized for him the same kind of undercover operation as I did in Chicago, continued with my job, and was with the movies for about four years until 1938. Treatment-- showed them how to write a report. What shot, what were things that are important? Don't get yourself into the middle of anything, and I kept doing this at night and weekends. And in between, I also married Avina. I'm glad you have the chance to meet her.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, shortly-- no, no, no, yeah. It's 1938. One of the guys says, Joe, you ought to have a little-- no-- yeah-- Joe, you ought to be a little more familiar with the Nazi headquarters so that if we see this or that, it has some meaning to you. You ought to go to one of those meetings. So when I announced to Avina that I'm going to go there, she immediately announced she's going to go with me and protect me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the German House at 634 West 15th Street near Figueroa. It turned out to be the first showing of the first German newsreel of the annexation of Austria. They called it Anschluss. You know how to write that, Anschluss. So we went. We were to give the Nazi salute at the beginning, then came the news reel. And the only way I could describe it is it shows on the sidewalks absolutely full. You couldn't get another human being in between one and the other and Hitler coming down the main boulevard of Vienna called the Ring, R-I-N-G. And the people reacting as if Jesus Christ himself came off of the cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came home. And Avina knew what was in my head. You've got to give all of your life to fighting the Nazis. We talked and talked, and then she disappeared. I should say, she was a nurse before I married her, in Chicago. She was a nurse in Chicago, but I married her here. And she comes back with a uniform on, the nurse's uniform. And I said, what's that idea. She says, you are going to quit, and you're going to do what you want to do. Because on the salary that Mendel Silberberg offered, we could not have made it. So that's when I became, became completely active in the fight against the Nazis. I gave it a name. It became known as the Community Relations Committee. Later on it became the Community Relations Committee of the Jewish Federation Council of greater Los Angeles. It was the first community relations committee in the country. Now there are 117 community relations committees or community relations councils.

speaker: interviewee
text: So having been sort of the founder and sort of-- I led the way. Maybe that's the best way to say it. I don't like to see 'leader.' It's against my nature. I led the way, and now you have all of those.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, the anti-defamation league consisted-- I know, I'm going back to Chicago now. At that time, the anti-defamation league consisted of two men and one secretary. National headquarters in Chicago on Wells Street where the elevated was running. And every time the elevated came, the whole house shook. Today the anti-defamation league is doing great, great work. And I'm proud of what they are accomplishing. The American Jewish Committee, the other well-known national Jewish organization, was a-- kind that said, don't rock the boat. Don't rock the boat because if we make too much noise against Hitler, they are going to take it out on the Jews in Germany. And it took a long time to make them discover what kind of games were being played. That tells you now how I got into the racket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you want to ask a question? Or shall I continue? Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I became an indirect admirer of Dr. Paul Joseph Goebbels, G-O-E-B-B-E-L-S. What do I mean by that? I had no use for that dirty so-and-so. But I think he was the most clever, the ablest propaganda operator in the world. His job was to make Nazism accepted all over the world, to soften up the soft underbelly of what they considered to be the soft underbelly of the likely enemies of Nazi Germany. I'll show you one thing. You can read the headline. You can't see the picture very well. The picture shows what appears to be a rabbi killing a Christian child to use its blood in the matzos, which those horrible Jews are eating on Passover. With this-- with this, you can about imagine how people reacted. I'll show you another one. I used to publish-- once I went to work for the Community Relations Committee, I thought, all of this information we have here. We've got to do something. We've got to do something. We've got to make the world know about it. Just the same thinking as I had when we started to print the anti-Nazi newspaper in Chicago. And I started publishing, what was called, the We printed only 1,000 copies. But we reached millions because they went to all kinds of paper, the Washington paper, all of them. He used a lot of this stuff, exposes of the Nazi activities, primarily of this country. He out there had a story about-- one full issue on what the Nazis were doing in Mexico and South Africa and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's one that helped me really do about something. It says, why do we fight Marxism? You need to turn it this way a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do we fight Marxism? This is the cover of a brochure that we picked up through our agents at the Nazi headquarters here. And one fine day, the reporters, the undercover reporters, told me one after the other-- none of them knowing each other as working for me-- all of the anti-communist stuff is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schlieffen Plan it said up here. What is the Schlieffen Plan? General von Schlieffen was one of the high ranking generals in World War I-- of Germany in World War I. He preached, never fight a war on two fronts. All of a sudden, Mr. Litvinov and Ribbentrop get together and sign a non-aggression pact. What's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the other issue of the I predicted that this plan is going to come. And this is bound to lead to war. Now first, Hitler-- first Hitler marched into the Rhineland, which, according to the Versailles Treaty, was demilitarized. He couldn't have any soldiers, any weapons there. He violated that. And they let him get away with it. Before that, Mussolini thumbed his nose at the League of Nations by attacking Ethiopia. Next Hitler was crying crocodile tears for the Sudeten Germans in Czechoslovakia. I predicted Hitler would make war against Czechoslovakia. I was wrong in my prediction. But he browbeat Mr. Chamberlain into giving, handing it to him at Munich. And then finally, came the attack upon Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But while all of this was going on, I need for you to describe for me what you were doing here in Los Angeles. Less important is what was going on in Europe. I need for you to tell me about the activities of the Nazis locally and what you did to fight them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, primarily it was, stay away from international affairs with the exception of Germany. Don't say anything against Russia or anything else, but just make people believe that what Hitler is doing is going to save the world. Actually, did I say, Russia? I shouldn't have said, Russia. We were saving the world from the horrible communists. Then spreading the propaganda that Jews are our enemy, our enemy. They are the international bankers. And the next day, you can see one of their brochures or pamphlets saying, they are the international communist plotters, anything to make the Americans feel Nazi Germany is their champion, their fighter. That's one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other thing is to undermine belief in Roosevelt, whether you were a Republican or you were a Democrat, because Roosevelt was plotting to go to war against Germany. And who was behind them? Obviously the Jews. I mean, this was the continuous flow of propaganda, well organized, terribly well organized and very, very effective.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had people sitting in both houses of Congress, reprinting articles-- no, I'm saying it wrong. There was a Nazi propagandist agent. He was a Nazi agent and a propagandist by the name of George Sylvester Viereck. Viereck wrote pro-German speeches and had those speeches delivered on the floor of both houses of Congress, then reprints it, reprints from the congressional records made. And it's mailed out by the tens of thousands at congressional frank to people, wherever, to whatever mailing list they could contain. George Sylvester Viereck, that was his job. I was the one that finally convinced the FBI it was time to arrest, pardon me, that dirty so-and-so. I almost said the bad word. They finally arrested him, and he wound up in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- How were you-- But

speaker: interviewee
text: The main propaganda, the main objective was that. Espionage was completely separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved with espionage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I involved?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, some Nazis, but they were separate. They were told never go near the German House. Never going near the German House on 15th Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever, after the initial time that you went to the German House, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I actively do what?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the first time you went to the German House, did you yourself ever go back there again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was the only time I-- that's the only time I was there. But an attorney called me. He says, Joe, I'd like you to meet a fellow by the name of so-and-so. I told him to come and see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is it about? He says, that's all I'm going to say. So he knew what kind of work I was doing, and therefore understood, Joe sometimes shouldn't be told, whatever. I shouldn't talk on the telephone. Incidentally-- I forgot the Ku Klux Klan statue. You.

speaker: interviewee
text: A fellow came over. He talked like a, with an Italian accent, was involved as a detective in the Lindbergh kidnapping case. He says my house is wired. Every room is wired. On top of a garage, I have a room where the recording equipment is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see the people I had to see. I need x-thousand dollars. And most of the important Nazi meetings were never held at the German House, just the key people. Where were they held?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said he had his recording equipment on top of-- in a room on top of the garage. And every room was wired. And that's where the Nazis had the important meetings. And as quickly as things were transcribed, they went mostly to the FBI, a little less mostly to army intelligence, and before we got into the war, also to British intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you infiltrate the Nazis locally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we hired people who wanted American-- one was a full-time employee. The others were people who were employed in legitimate jobs and did this weekends. Sometimes they didn't go to work when it was important and so on and so forth. And they were free-- they were paid. I met with them rarely. We had a room in a Flee Hotel, Flee, F-L-E-E Hotel on Main Street. The hotel was not very big, but there was a building to the left of it and there was a building to the right of it. And on top of it, you could walk from one side of the street to the other side of the street. So you could use three entrances, leave, or enter, or leave. I met with them there. And we had, we had understandings, how to meet, when to meet, how to reach each other, and so on, and so forth. And that's what reminded me of the Ku Klux Klan statue.

speaker: interviewee
text: She saw a statue of the Klansman. And I was going to have it here, but I forgot. Once, one of my undercover men, who was the chief intelligence officer of the Ku Klux Klan, reported that the Klan was going to scare the hell out of those Jew producers at the studios. On the desk of every one of the Louis B. Mayer-- what is Cohn's first name? Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Harry Cohn, Jack Warner, there would be a little statue. On the bottom it says, KGYI, Klansman I Greet You.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here because we have to change the reel, and we'll get the-- and we're going to get the statue.
